cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are diving deep into a book that will genuinely change the way you think about every decision you make, from the tiny ones to the ones that shape your whole life.
The book is called Thinking in Bets: Making Smarter Decisions When You Don't Have All the Facts, and it is written by Annie Duke.
Now, before Annie Duke was an author and a decision strategist, she was something a lot more unusual.
She was a professional poker player, one of the best in the world, and she did it after spending years studying cognitive psychology in graduate school.
So what you get in this book is a rare combination.

You get someone who understands, at the deepest academic level, how the human brain forms beliefs and makes mistakes, and you also get someone who spent two decades sitting at a table risking real money on incomplete information, where being fooled by your own mind costs you cash, immediately and painfully.
That combination is what makes this book so powerful.
She is not theorizing about uncertainty from the safety of a lecture hall.
She lived inside it.
And she is going to teach you to live inside it more gracefully too.
Let me start where she starts, because it is the perfect story.
Take yourself back to February of 2015.
Super Bowl forty nine.
The Seattle Seahawks are playing the New England Patriots.
There are twenty six seconds left in the game.
The Seahawks are down by four points, and they have the ball on the New England one yard line.
One yard.
They need to get that ball across the goal line to win the Super Bowl.
And here is the thing that made everybody feel like the outcome was already decided.
The Seahawks had Marshawn Lynch in their backfield, one of the most punishing, unstoppable running backs in the entire league, a man they nicknamed Beast Mode.
Everyone watching, and I mean everyone, assumed the same thing.
You hand the ball to Marshawn Lynch, he powers it one yard forward, and you win the Super Bowl.
It seemed obvious.
So what does Pete Carroll, the Seahawks head coach, do?
He calls a pass play.
And the pass gets intercepted by a rookie defender named Malcolm Butler.
The Patriots win the Super Bowl.
And within seconds, the entire sports world lost its mind.
The headlines were brutal.
People called it the worst play call in Super Bowl history.
The worst play call in the history of the National Football League.
Sportswriters, fans, analysts, everyone agreed.
Pete Carroll was an idiot.
He had cost his team the championship with an inexplicably stupid decision.
But Annie Duke asks you to pause right there and think about something.
Was it actually a stupid decision?
And here is where she pulls the whole book open.
She points out that the outrage was not really about the decision at all.
The outrage was about the result.

If Malcolm Butler had been a half step slower, if that pass had been completed for a touchdown, the exact same play call would have been celebrated as a brilliant, gutsy, championship winning piece of strategy.
Nobody would have said Pete Carroll was an idiot.
They would have said he was a genius.
The play call did not change.
Only the outcome changed.
And when you actually look at the decision on its own merits, it was defensible, maybe even smart.
There were twenty six seconds left and the Seahawks only had one timeout.
If they ran the ball and did not score, the clock might run out before they could get another play off.
Throwing the ball was a way to essentially get an extra play for free, because an incomplete pass stops the clock.
On top of that, the odds of an interception on that specific type of pass play were extremely low, historically something like one or two percent.
So Pete Carroll made a decision that gave his team more chances to score and carried a tiny risk of disaster.
The tiny risk happened.
That is bad luck.
That is not a bad decision.
This is the single most important idea in the whole book, and I want you to sit with it, especially if you are driving right now or grinding through a set at the gym.
There is a difference between the quality of a decision and the quality of the outcome.
They are two separate things.
A good decision can lead to a bad outcome.
A bad decision can lead to a good outcome.
And most of us, almost all the time, judge our decisions purely by how they turned out.
Annie Duke has a name for this mistake, a term she borrows from the poker world.
She calls it resulting.
Resulting is when you look at the result of a decision and use that result to judge whether the decision itself was good or bad.
And it is one of the most corrosive habits in human thinking, because it teaches you all the wrong lessons.
Think about how this plays out in your own life.
You had a couple of drinks at a party, and you decided to drive home anyway.
You made it home fine.
Nothing bad happened.
So your brain quietly files that away as, that was fine, no big deal.
But it was a terrible decision that happened to have a lucky outcome.
Or flip it around.

You did all your research, you interviewed carefully, you took a job at a company that looked fantastic on every measurable dimension, and then six months later the company got acquired and your whole department was laid off.
Bad outcome.
But was it a bad decision?
Probably not.
You made a smart choice and got unlucky.
If you let the outcome convince you that you are a bad decision maker, you will start second guessing exactly the kind of good process that will serve you well over a lifetime.
So the first great gift of this book is learning to decouple, to separate, the quality of your decisions from the quality of your outcomes.
Because here is the uncomfortable truth.
In the short run, you cannot control your outcomes.
You can only control your decisions.
Outcomes are a mix of your decision quality and a big, messy factor called luck.
And the only thing you actually get to steer is the decision.

So if you want to get better at life, you have to get better at evaluating decisions on their own terms, before you know how they turned out, and even after, refusing to let a good or bad result fool you about the quality of the process.
Now, why is this so hard?
Why do we fall into resulting so naturally?
To answer that, Annie Duke gives us maybe the most useful metaphor in the book.
She says most of us think life is like chess, but life is actually like poker.
Let me unpack that, because it matters enormously.
In chess, there is no hidden information.
Every piece is right there on the board for both players to see.
There are no dice, no shuffled deck, no luck of the draw.
If you lose a game of chess, it is almost entirely because you made worse decisions than your opponent.
The outcome maps very cleanly onto the quality of your play.
Chess is a game of pure skill and complete information.
And because chess works that way, it is reasonable in chess to judge a decision by its outcome, because luck barely enters the picture.
But life is not chess.
Life is poker.
In poker, there is enormous hidden information.
You cannot see your opponent's cards.
You do not know what card is coming next off the deck.
You are constantly making decisions based on incomplete information, forced to bet on what is probably true without ever being certain.
And crucially, poker has luck baked right into it.
You can play a hand absolutely perfectly, make every correct decision, get your money in when you are a heavy favorite to win, and still lose because the last card betrays you.
And you can play a hand terribly, make every wrong move, and still win because you got lucky.
Over a single hand, or a single night, luck can completely swamp skill.
It is only over thousands and thousands of hands that skill reliably rises to the top.
Once you truly absorb that life is poker and not chess, everything shifts.
You realize that the way your life turns out is determined by exactly two things.
The quality of your decisions, and luck.
That is it.
Those are the two inputs.
And here is the trap, the psychological trap that Annie Duke says almost every human being falls into.
We are wired to take credit for our good outcomes and blame our bad outcomes on luck.
When things go well, we say, that was my skill, my hard work, my brilliant judgment.
When things go badly, we say, oh, that was just bad luck, the deck was stacked against me, it was out of my hands.
Psychologists call this self serving bias, and it is enormously flattering and enormously destructive.
Because if you credit all your wins to skill and blame all your losses on luck, you can never learn anything.
You never examine the good decisions that got lucky, and you never examine the bad decisions that you brushed off as misfortune.
You have built yourself a closed loop where you are always right and the universe is always to blame.
Annie Duke was forced out of that loop, because in poker the feedback is relentless and the money is real.
She had to develop the discipline to ask, after a winning hand, did I actually play that well or did I just get lucky?
And after a losing hand, was that really bad luck, or did I make a mistake I need to fix?
That question, asked honestly, over and over, is the engine of getting better at anything.
So how do we get better at this?
How do we train ourselves to stop resulting and start thinking clearly under uncertainty?
Annie Duke's answer, the beating heart of the whole book, is beautifully simple.
Treat your beliefs as bets.
Treat your decisions as bets.
That is the reframe.
Every time you make a decision, you are placing a bet on a particular future, wagering your time, your money, your energy, your reputation, against an uncertain outcome.
And every belief you hold is really just a bet on what you think is true about the world.
Now, why does thinking in terms of bets help so much?
Because a bet forces honesty.
Let me explain how she gets there, because it involves understanding something a little embarrassing about how our brains actually form beliefs.
You probably imagine that you form beliefs like a scientist.
You imagine that you hear a claim, you gather evidence, you weigh it carefully, and then, only after you have vetted it, you decide whether or not to believe it.
That is the ideal.
That is how we like to picture ourselves.
But that is not how the human brain works at all.
Research shows that the actual sequence is nearly the opposite.
We hear something, and we believe it, automatically, instantly, by default.
Belief comes first.
The vetting, the questioning, the checking against reality, that only happens later, if it happens at all, and only if we have the time and the motivation to do it.
Most of the time we simply do not bother.

So we walk around with a head full of beliefs that we absorbed without ever examining, beliefs we picked up from a headline, a friend, a thing we saw once, a story that fit what we already wanted to think.
And it gets worse, because of something called motivated reasoning.
Once we hold a belief, we do not evaluate new information neutrally.
We evaluate it in a way that protects the belief we already have.
Evidence that confirms what we believe gets waved right through.
Evidence that contradicts what we believe gets picked apart, doubted, dismissed, or ignored.
We are not truth seeking machines by default.
We are belief defending machines.

And the smarter you are, Annie Duke points out with a certain grim humor, the better you often are at this, because intelligence just gives you more sophisticated tools to rationalize whatever you already wanted to believe.
Being smart does not protect you from motivated reasoning.
Sometimes it makes it worse.
So here is where the magic of the bet comes in.
Imagine you say something at a dinner party, some confident claim, and someone across the table looks at you and says, wanna bet?
Wanna bet?
What happens in your brain the instant you hear those two words?
Everything changes.
Suddenly you are not just casually asserting something to feel good and fit in.
Suddenly there is money on the line, and you are forced to actually examine how sure you really are.
You start running through the evidence.
Where did I hear this?
Is that source reliable?
Have I actually checked this, or am I just repeating something?
Am I really eighty percent confident, or is it more like sixty, or honestly maybe I have no idea?
That question, wanna bet, is like a spotlight that snaps on and illuminates exactly how flimsy or how solid your belief actually is.
It forces calibration.
It forces you to be honest about what you actually know versus what you just feel like you know.
And Annie Duke's suggestion is that you learn to ask yourself, wanna bet, before anyone else has to ask it for you.
Make it an internal habit.
Every time you catch yourself asserting something with confidence, quietly ask, if I had to put money on this, real money, how much would I bet, and at what odds?
That single move drains a huge amount of unearned certainty out of your thinking.
It turns you from a person who is casually, sloppily sure of things into a person who is carefully, honestly calibrated.
This leads directly to one of the most liberating ideas in the book, and it is about the phrase, I'm not sure.
Most of us are taught, especially in professional life, that saying I'm not sure is a sign of weakness.
That confident people always have the answer, that leaders project certainty, that admitting uncertainty makes you look incompetent or wishy washy.
Annie Duke wants to blow that up completely.
She argues that I'm not sure is not a weakness.
It is a strength.
It is, in fact, a more accurate description of reality, because you almost never are completely sure.
The world is uncertain.
Hidden information is everywhere.

So the person who says I'm not sure, but here is my best estimate and here is how confident I am, is being far more honest and far more useful than the person who projects false, black and white certainty.
She wants you to get comfortable expressing your beliefs not in absolutes, not in yes or no, right or wrong, black or white, but in probabilities and degrees of confidence.
Instead of saying, this project will succeed, you say, I think there is about a seventy percent chance this project succeeds, and here is what would move that number up or down.
Instead of saying, that new hire is going to be great, you say, I am maybe sixty percent confident this hire works out well.
This is a radically different way of talking and thinking, and it does something wonderful.
It makes room for reality.
When you assign a seventy percent probability to something and it does not happen, you are not shattered, because you already knew there was a thirty percent chance it would not.
You built the uncertainty into your worldview from the start.
You are not blindsided.
You are not forced into the self serving story that it was all bad luck, because you already acknowledged the odds.
Annie Duke even redefines what it means to be confident.
In her world, confidence is not, I am one hundred percent certain and I will hear no argument.
Confidence is calibration.
It is the ability to accurately communicate how sure you are on a scale.

A truly confident person, in her sense, can say, I am ninety percent sure about this one, but only forty percent sure about that one, and they can tell the difference, and they update those numbers honestly as new evidence arrives.
That is real confidence.
It is intellectual honesty about the state of your own knowledge, not a performance of certainty you do not actually possess.
And this reframe also lets you redefine being wrong, which is huge, because our fear of being wrong is one of the biggest obstacles to clear thinking.
If you live in a black and white world, being wrong is a catastrophe.
You said yes, the answer was no, you were wrong, you failed, you look stupid.
But if you live in a probabilistic world, being wrong looks completely different.
If you said there was a seventy percent chance of something and the thirty percent outcome happened, you were not simply wrong.
Your estimate may have been perfectly good.
The unlikely thing just happened, the way unlikely things sometimes do.
Being wrong becomes not a source of shame but a source of information, a chance to check whether your probability estimate was miscalibrated and needs adjusting.
You go from fearing wrongness to being curious about it.
And that curiosity is what lets you actually learn and improve, instead of getting defensive and learning nothing.
Now, let me get to what Annie Duke identifies as the real enemy in all of this.
Because you might be thinking, okay, this all sounds great, treat beliefs as bets, express things in probabilities, get comfortable with uncertainty.
Why is it so hard?
Why do we resist it so fiercely?
And her answer is that the enemy of good decision making is our need to protect our self image, our ego.
Our identity gets wrapped up in being right.

When you have staked your sense of yourself on being a smart, competent, correct person, then admitting uncertainty feels like a threat, admitting you were wrong feels like an attack on who you are, and questioning your own beliefs feels like betraying yourself.
So your brain does everything it can to protect the ego, and truth seeking gets sacrificed on the altar of feeling good about yourself.
This is why willpower alone is not enough.
You cannot simply decide to be more rational and expect it to stick, because you are fighting against deeply wired machinery whose whole job is to make you feel good and feel right.
Annie Duke is very clear eyed about this.
She says trying to be perfectly rational on your own, by sheer force of will, is basically a losing battle.
The bias is too strong, the ego is too protective, and you cannot see your own blind spots precisely because they are blind spots.
So what is the solution?
Her answer is that you cannot do this alone.
You need other people.
Specifically, you need to build or join what she calls a truth seeking group, sometimes called a decision pod or an accountability group.
This is a small group of people who have agreed, explicitly, that the goal of the group is accuracy, not comfort.
That the group rewards truth over agreement, honesty over feeling good.
In her own life, Annie Duke was part of exactly such a group of professional poker players.
They would share their hands with each other, and the whole point was not to make each other feel good about how they played.
The whole point was to find the mistakes.
To get closer to the truth about what the right decision actually was.
It was uncomfortable sometimes, but it made every one of them better.
She grounds this idea in some serious intellectual history.
She draws on the work of a sociologist named Robert Merton, who studied the norms of science, the unwritten rules that make science actually work as a truth seeking enterprise.
Merton captured these norms in an acronym, CUDOS.
Let me walk you through it, because each piece is a design principle for your own truth seeking group.
The C stands for communism, and here it does not mean anything political.
It means communism of data, the idea that all the data should be shared.
You do not get to cherry pick and hide the facts that make you look bad.
Everyone in the group has to put the full picture on the table, the whole hand, not just the flattering parts.
The U stands for universalism, which means you evaluate a claim on its own merits, regardless of who is making it.
You do not accept an idea because it came from someone important or reject it because it came from someone you dislike.
The message is judged separately from the messenger.
The D and the O stand for disinterestedness, which means you try to guard against the conflicts of interest and the personal stakes that bias your judgment.
You are aware that you want certain answers to be true, and you actively work against that pull.

And the S stands for organized skepticism, which means the group makes a discipline out of questioning and testing ideas, inviting dissent, actively looking for what might be wrong rather than nodding along.
That principle of separating the messenger from the message is worth pausing on, because it cuts both ways in daily life.
We reject good ideas because we do not like the person who said them.
We accept bad ideas because we admire or trust the person who said them.
A real truth seeking group trains you to focus on the accuracy of the claim itself, not on your feelings about the source, and not on whether the group agrees.
Agreement is not the goal.
Accuracy is the goal.
Sometimes the most valuable person in the room is the one willing to disagree, the one who invites the dissent that everyone else was too polite or too comfortable to raise.
And Annie Duke warns you about the opposite of a truth seeking group, which is what most of us actually do when we face a hard decision or a bad outcome.
We go to our friends, and we tell them a very particular version of the story.
We share the outcome, and we frame it in the way most likely to get us sympathy and validation.
My boss was so unfair to me.
Can you believe what my ex did.
This deal fell through and it was totally not my fault.
And what do our friends do, because they love us and want us to feel good?
They validate.
They say, oh, you poor thing, you did everything right, it was all them.
And that feels wonderful, and it is completely useless.
It teaches you nothing.
It just reinforces the self serving story you walked in with.
Empty validation is the enemy of growth.
A real accountability partner, by contrast, is someone who will say, okay, but tell me the whole story, tell me your part in it, what could you have done differently.
That is uncomfortable, and it is the only thing that actually helps.
So one of the most practical takeaways from the book is this.
Go find, or build, even a small pod of people, maybe just two or three, who agree to hold each other to accuracy rather than comfort.
People with whom you share the full picture, including the parts that make you look bad, and who you explicitly invite to poke holes in your reasoning.
That group will make you smarter than any amount of solo willpower ever could, because they can see your blind spots even when you cannot.
Now, Annie Duke shifts the book toward a set of practical mental tools, techniques you can actually use, and these are gold.
The first one deals with a problem she calls the battle between your past, present, and future selves.
Here is the issue.
There is a version of you that exists right now, the present self, and there are versions of you that will exist in the future, the future selves.
And the present self, frankly, is kind of a jerk to the future selves.
The present self wants the reward now and does not much care about the consequences later.
This is why you eat the whole pizza tonight even though tomorrow morning you will regret it.
This is why you skip the workout, blow the budget, hit the snooze button, send the angry email.
The present self is grabbing the immediate payoff and sticking the future self with the bill.
Psychologists call this temporal discounting.
We discount, we shrink down, the value of things that happen in the future compared to things that happen right now.

The future feels abstract and distant and not quite real, so we systematically favor the present self at the future self's expense, even when we know, intellectually, that the future self matters just as much.

Annie Duke wants to give you tools to bring the future self into the room, to make that distant version of you feel real and present at the moment of decision, so that you actually take their interests into account.
The first tool she offers is a beautiful old story, and it gives us a term she uses throughout the book.
It is the story of Ulysses and the sirens, from Homer's ancient Greek epic.

Ulysses is sailing home, and he has to pass an island where the sirens live, creatures whose song is so irresistibly beautiful that any sailor who hears it is compelled to steer toward them and crash on the rocks and die.
Ulysses wants to hear the song, he is curious, but he knows that in the moment, under the spell of that music, he will not be able to resist.
So what does he do?
Ahead of time, while he is still thinking clearly, he makes a plan.

He orders his sailors to plug their own ears with wax so they cannot hear the song, and then he orders them to tie him to the mast of the ship, and he commands them that no matter how much he begs and pleads to be released once the song starts, they must not untie him until they are safely past.
And that is exactly what happens.
The song comes, Ulysses is driven mad with desire, he screams to be let loose, and his sailors, ears plugged, just tie him tighter and row on.
And they survive.
Annie Duke calls this a Ulysses contract.
It is a decision you make in advance, in a moment of clarity, that binds your future self and protects you from the moment of weakness you know is coming.
It is precommitment.
It is tying yourself to the mast.
And you can build Ulysses contracts all over your life.
If you know that late at night you will raid the kitchen, do not keep the junk food in the house, so that the future self simply cannot make the bad choice.
If you know you will impulse buy, remove your saved credit card from the shopping sites.
If you know you will hit snooze, put the alarm across the room.
If you tend to fire off angry emails, set a rule that anything written in anger sits in drafts overnight.
Each of these is your present self, thinking clearly, reaching forward to protect a future self who will be tempted, tired, or emotional.
You are not relying on willpower in the dangerous moment.
You are removing the need for willpower by making the decision earlier, when you were calm.
The second tool for bridging present and future is one you can do in your head in about thirty seconds, and it is called ten ten ten.
When you are facing a decision, especially one where you feel a strong pull toward an immediate reward, you ask yourself three questions.
How will I feel about this decision ten minutes from now?
How will I feel about it ten months from now?
And how will I feel about it ten years from now?
Those three time horizons pull your future selves right into the present.
Maybe eating the whole cake feels amazing for ten minutes.
But how does it feel ten months into a health goal you keep sabotaging?
Maybe snapping at your kid relieves your frustration for ten minutes, but how does that pattern look in ten years?
Ten ten ten stretches your time perspective and lets the future self get a vote, right there at the moment of choice.
Then Annie Duke gives us a pair of related tools that are about mentally traveling into the future to make better decisions today.
The first is called backcasting.
Backcasting is where you imagine that you have already succeeded.

You project yourself forward to a future where your goal has been fully achieved, where everything worked out beautifully, and then you look backward from that imagined success and ask, how did we get here?
What were the steps that led to this outcome?
What had to happen along the way?
Backcasting helps you build a concrete roadmap by starting from the destination and working back toward where you are now.
But here is the tool that Annie Duke says is even more powerful, and it is the one I most want you to remember, because it uses something surprising.
It is called a premortem.
Now, you have probably heard of a postmortem, which is what you do after something dies, after a project fails, when you gather around and figure out what went wrong.
A premortem does the exact same analysis, except you do it before, at the start, while you can still change the outcome.
Here is how it works.
You imagine yourself in the future, and you imagine that your decision or your project has failed.
Completely failed.
It went badly.
And then you ask, why?
What went wrong?
What were the reasons it failed?
You force yourself to generate all the ways this could go off the rails.
Why is this so much more powerful than just optimistically planning for success?
Because of how our brains work.
When we are excited about a plan, our motivated reasoning kicks in, and we do not want to think about failure.
It feels disloyal to the plan, even a little like jinxing it.
So we suppress all the doubts and the risks, and we march forward blind to the very dangers that will sink us.
The premortem gives you permission, even an assignment, to think negatively.
By imagining the failure has already happened, you free your mind to surface all the risks you would otherwise have swept under the rug.

People who are afraid to voice a concern about a beloved plan will happily explain, in a premortem, why the plan failed, because you have made it a game, you have made it safe, you have even made it fun.
Annie Duke describes this as harnessing the positive power of negative thinking.
You use pessimism deliberately, as a tool, to surface the risks while there is still time to prepare for them, to build in safeguards, to adjust the plan, to set expectations.
The premortem does not make you gloomy.
It makes you prepared.
And combined with backcasting, imagining both the success and the failure, you get a much richer, much more realistic picture of the range of futures you are actually betting on.
Which brings us to another key concept, scenario planning, or thinking in terms of a tree of possible futures.
Most of us, when we make a decision, imagine a single story about how it will play out.
We picture one future, usually the one we are hoping for, and we treat it as if it is the future.
But that is not how reality works.
Any decision opens up a whole branching tree of possible futures, some more likely than others.
Good decision makers, Annie Duke says, hold that whole tree in mind.
They ask, what are all the different ways this could unfold?
And roughly how probable is each branch?
Instead of betting on one expected story, you map the range of outcomes and their likelihoods.
This does something important for you emotionally.
When an unlikely bad branch actually happens, you are not devastated and you do not fall into resulting, because you already knew that branch existed and had a probability.
You had already, in a sense, made peace with it.
You bet on the whole tree, not on a single leaf, so no single outcome can fool you into thinking your decision was bad.
Now I want to talk about an enemy that lives inside your own body, because Annie Duke gives it real attention.
In poker it is called tilt.
Tilt is the emotional state, usually triggered by a bad beat, a run of bad luck, a frustrating loss, where your emotions hijack your judgment and you start making terrible decisions.
A player on tilt is angry or rattled or desperate, and they start playing recklessly, chasing losses, abandoning all the discipline that normally guides them.
And here is the cruel part.
When you are on tilt, you usually do not know you are on tilt.
It feels like you are thinking clearly.
It feels like the world is being unfair and you are just responding sensibly.
But you are not.
Your decision making has been compromised by emotion, and every choice you make in that state is suspect.
We all go on tilt, far outside of poker.
You get one piece of infuriating news and then you make a snap decision in a rage that you regret for years.
You get your feelings hurt and you fire back with something cutting.
You get anxious and you make a fear driven choice you would never make when calm.
Annie Duke's advice is twofold.
First, learn to recognize the signs of your own tilt, the physical and emotional signals that tell you your judgment is currently unreliable.
Maybe your heart is racing, maybe you feel that hot flush of anger, maybe you notice you are telling yourself a story where you are the pure victim and someone else is pure villain.
Learn your tells.
And second, when you recognize tilt, the rule is simple.
Do not decide.
Step away.
Take yourself out of the game until the emotional storm passes and your clear thinking returns.
The best poker players, when they feel tilt coming on, will get up and leave the table, because they know that no decision they make right now can be trusted.
You can build this into your life as a Ulysses contract too.
Make a rule, in advance, that you do not make important decisions when you are furious, terrified, or heartbroken.
You wait.
You sleep on it.
You let the tilt drain out first.
There is one more bias Annie Duke wants to arm you against, and it ties back to everything we have discussed.
It is called hindsight bias, and it is the way memory rewrites the past.
After an outcome happens, our brains quietly go back and edit our memory of what we knew and thought beforehand.
Once we know how something turned out, we become convinced that we knew it all along, that the outcome was obvious, that we saw it coming.
This is why, after the Seahawks threw that interception, everyone was suddenly certain that it was obviously a terrible call, when beforehand it would not have struck most people as crazy at all.
Hindsight bias makes the past look more predictable than it actually was.
And it is poison for learning, because it prevents you from honestly evaluating your decisions.

If you remember yourself as having known the outcome all along, you can neither give yourself credit for a genuinely good decision that got unlucky nor honestly examine a bad decision, because your memory has already been doctored to fit the result.

This is one of the strongest arguments for keeping some kind of decision journal, writing down what you actually believed and how confident you were before you knew the outcome, so that your later self cannot rewrite the record.
When you can go back and see, in your own words, what you really thought at the time, hindsight bias loses its power to fool you.
So let me pull all of this together, because there is a single, coherent worldview underneath all these tools.
Annie Duke is teaching you that good decision making is a skill, not a personality trait you are born with or without.
It is something you can practice and build, deliberately, over a lifetime.
And the way you build it rests on a few pillars.
You embrace uncertainty instead of pretending it away, getting comfortable saying I'm not sure and thinking in probabilities rather than false certainties.
You decouple your decisions from your outcomes, judging the quality of your process on its own merits and refusing to let a lucky win or an unlucky loss teach you the wrong lesson.
And you bet on your beliefs, treating every conviction as a wager that forces you to be honest about how much you actually know.

You accept that life is poker, not chess, a game of incomplete information and luck where the best decision can still lose and a terrible one can still win, and where the only rational strategy is to keep making the best bets you can and let the results, over the long run, take care of themselves.
The people who thrive under uncertainty are not the ones who are certain.

They are the ones who have made peace with not being certain, who hold their beliefs loosely enough to update them, who surround themselves with people who tell them the truth instead of what they want to hear, and who evaluate themselves by the quality of their bets rather than the luck of their draws.
That is what it means to think in bets.
So let me leave you with something concrete, a set of things you can actually start doing this week to become a better bettor on your own life.
Do not just nod along and forget this.
Pick a few of these and put them into motion.
First, this week, catch yourself resulting.
Every time you find yourself judging a decision, yours or somebody else's, by how it turned out, stop and ask, but was that actually a good decision given what they knew at the time?
Separate the decision from the outcome, out loud if you can.
Just noticing how often you conflate the two will start to rewire your thinking.

Second, before the week is over, take one belief you hold strongly, something you are sure about, maybe about your work, your finances, your relationships, or the wider world, and ask yourself, wanna bet?
If I had to put real money on this, how confident am I actually?
Put a number on it.
Seventy percent?
Ninety?
And then ask what evidence you are actually resting that number on.
You will be surprised how many of your certainties turn out to be flimsier than they felt.
Third, start expressing your beliefs in probabilities, at least to yourself, for the rest of the week.
Instead of thinking this will definitely work or this will never work, force yourself to assign a rough percentage.
Get comfortable with the discomfort of not being black and white.
This is a muscle, and it strengthens fast once you start using it.
Fourth, find your pod.
Identify at least one person in your life who you trust to tell you the truth rather than just make you feel good, and have an honest conversation with them about a real decision you are facing.
Give them the whole picture, including the parts that make you look bad, and explicitly ask them to poke holes in your thinking.
Invite the dissent.
If you do not have such a person, start being that person for someone else, and you will attract them.
Fifth, run a premortem on something real this week.
Pick a plan or a decision that is currently in motion, a project, a purchase, a change you are making, and sit down and imagine that six months from now it has completely failed.
Then write down every reason why.
Do not censor yourself.
Once you have that list of failure modes, you will almost certainly spot a risk or two you can defuse right now, while there is still time.
Sixth, build one Ulysses contract.
Find one recurring moment of weakness in your life, one place where your present self keeps betraying your future self, and set up a system in advance that removes the temptation or binds your hands.
Tie yourself to the mast in one small, concrete way, and notice how much easier it is than fighting the same battle of willpower over and over.

And seventh, the next time you feel that hot rush of tilt, anger, panic, wounded pride, recognize it as what it is, a signal that your judgment is temporarily unreliable, and make no important decision until it passes.
Just step away from the table.
Sleep on it.
Let the storm blow through, and decide tomorrow.
If you do even a few of these things consistently, you will slowly become a different kind of decision maker.
Calmer, clearer, more honest with yourself, and far less at the mercy of luck and ego.
You will stop torturing yourself over outcomes you could not control and start pouring your energy into the one thing you can control, the quality of your bets.
Because that is the whole game.
Life deals you the cards.
Luck decides a lot.
But over the long run, over thousands of hands, the quality of your decisions is what separates the people who thrive from the people who just keep blaming the deck.
Annie Duke spent twenty years learning that at a poker table with real money on the line, and in Thinking in Bets she is handing it to you for free.
The only thing left is to start playing your hands a little better, one honest bet at a time.
Good luck out there, and remember, when you catch yourself feeling certain, just quietly ask yourself, wanna bet?
